params.text:
Reporting exposure and ill health

Even with good controls, things can go wrong. If you are splashed, breathe in fumes, suffer a needlestick injury, or notice signs of ill health such as a rash, sore hands, coughing or breathlessness, tell your manager and record it. Early reporting means you can get first aid or medical advice and the problem can be put right before someone else is harmed.

Certain work-related illnesses and serious incidents caused by hazardous substances must be reported to the Health and Safety Executive under RIDDOR (the Reporting of Injuries, Diseases and Dangerous Occurrences Regulations 2013). This includes conditions such as occupational dermatitis, occupational asthma and certain dangerous exposures. Your organisation will have a process for making these reports.

  • Report exposures, near misses and symptoms promptly.
  • Follow first-aid guidance from the safety data sheet.
  • Your employer decides what must be reported under RIDDOR – but you must raise it so they can act.

Putting it all together

Good COSHH practice is a cycle: identify the hazardous substances, read the labels and safety data sheets, follow the COSHH assessment and hierarchy of control, store, use and dispose of substances safely, deal with spills correctly, and report any exposure or ill health so that lessons are learned. Doing this keeps you, your colleagues and the people you support safe and well.

params.question: A colleague has developed a sore, cracked rash on their hands after months of using cleaning products. What should happen?

answers.text: They report it to their manager, who considers whether it needs reporting under RIDDOR and reviews the controls

tipsAndFeedback.chosenFeedback: Correct. Occupational dermatitis is reportable under RIDDOR, and reporting it also prompts a review of gloves, hand care and the COSHH assessment.

answers.text: They ignore it because sore hands are just part of the job

tipsAndFeedback.chosenFeedback: No. Skin damage is a sign that controls are not working and should never be accepted as normal.

answers.text: They keep using the products without gloves so their skin toughens up

tipsAndFeedback.chosenFeedback: No. Continued exposure will make the condition worse; the cause must be identified and controlled.
